items.correct: A series of chemical reactions in cells that break down glucose to release energy as ATP.
items.misconception: Respiration means breathing — inhaling oxygen and exhaling carbon dioxide.
items.random_wrong: Respiration is the process by which plants make their own food using sunlight.
items.why: Everyday English equates 'respiration' with breathing; students rarely separate gas exchange from cellular metabolism.

items.q: What does the 'all-or-nothing' principle mean for action potentials?
items.correct: Once threshold is reached, the action potential fires at full size — sub-threshold stimuli produce no action potential.
items.misconception: Stronger stimuli produce larger action potentials.
items.random_wrong: Action potentials get bigger as they travel along the axon.
items.why: Students think 'more stimulus = bigger response' (analogous to volume control), missing that stimulus strength is coded by FREQUENCY of action potentials, not amplitude.

items.q: How does sweating help cool you down?
items.correct: Evaporation of sweat absorbs energy (latent heat) from the skin, lowering body temperature.
items.misconception: Sweat is cold and directly cools the skin on contact.
items.random_wrong: Sweating removes excess heat by carrying it out as liquid waste.
items.why: Students don't connect sweating to evaporation; they think the liquid itself is the cooling agent, not the phase change.

items.q: Which of these is a prokaryote?
items.why: It's Bacterium. “Human skin cell” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
The answer examiners want is: aerobic respiration. “protein synthesis” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: A bacterium is 2 µm long. How many fit end-to-end across a 1 mm gap?
items.why: Exact answer: 500 bacteria. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
